title: Untitled
research_problem: Developing a pedestrian microsimulation model for Chinese cities that accurately represents the high rate of signal non-compliance (red light running) and mixed traffic characteristics, as existing foreign micro-simulation tools (CORSIM, SIMTRAFFIC) fail to capture Chinese traffic conditions due to different behavioral patterns
method_summary: A pedestrian microsimulation model that classifies pedestrians into two types (law-obeying Type I and opportunistic Type II), uses questionnaires (495 respondents in Xi'an) to determine proportions and external factor influences, and extracts time gap distribution (DT) from videotape data (989 samples) to calibrate the critical gap acceptance criterion for pedestrian crossing decisions
datasets: Questionnaire data from 495 respondents in Xi'an (November 2003)
Videotape data from zebra crossing outside Southeast Gate of Xi'an Jiaotong University (989 samples)
baselines: CORSIM_and_SIMTRAFFIC (mentioned as not suitable for Chinese conditions)

main_claims.claim_text: The developed model successfully represents the high rate of pedestrian red light running (45-83% violation rate) and mixed traffic characteristics in Chinese cities

Classifying pedestrians into law-obeying and opportunistic types with dynamic proportions based on external factors (policeman presence, other pedestrians' behavior, vehicle flow) improves simulation realism
The time gap criterion (DT) for pedestrian crossing decisions can be effectively extracted from videotape data using the proposed calculation method

After parameter adjustment based on analyzing deviations between initial simulation results and field data, the simulation results agree well with field observations (mean red light runner proportion: 0.82 simulation vs 0.85 videotape)

limitations.text: The model only considers pedestrian decision making based on instantaneous states of oncoming vehicles; does not account for vehicle state changes (acceleration/deceleration) during crossing
Questionnaire respondents may not represent all pedestrian populations; self-reported behavior may differ from actual behavior
The DT calculation method needs improvement for turning vehicles to make the model more practical
Pedestrians' estimation of vehicle velocity cannot be directly extracted from videotape, requiring use of instantaneous speed as approximation

future_work: Extend model to handle pedestrian decision-making when oncoming vehicles change states (accelerate, decelerate)
Improve DT calculation method for turning vehicles
Validate model across multiple Chinese cities beyond Xi'an
Incorporate more sophisticated pedestrian following behavior dynamics
notes: Model parameters calibrated: r1=0.55 (proportion of law-obeying pedestrians), r2 adjusted from 0.69 to 0.35 based on validation; r3 derived from videotape gap acceptance data. Pedestrian average walking speed used: 1.2 m/s. Key finding: presence of other violating pedestrians has strong influence on signal non-compliance (30% report high/very high probability), while policeman presence has surprisingly low impact (only 2% high/very high).
